meeting_summary:
The Arvada City Council held a special meeting followed by a workshop on Tuesday, April 28, 2026. The special meeting addressed a second ethics investigation involving Councilmember Sharon Davis, who was excused from that portion. The council voted 4-2 to authorize retaining outside investigators and counsel for a new set of alleged ethics violations — the second such authorization since February. Councilmembers Griffith and Lovisone dissented, arguing the findings of the first investigation should be released before a new one is opened. City attorney Hoffman recommended proceeding with the second investigation simultaneously and handling both together for due process reasons.

The majority of the meeting was a workshop on the city's solid waste and recycling program. Staff presented proposed fee increases for the 2026 contract year: a 5-cent increase in the city program fee (to $1.74/month), maintaining the $1.06/month enterprise fee to fund the solid waste reserve, and pass-through increases from Republic Services tied to a contracted index. All council members present expressed support for these three fee-related items. The meeting also covered significant progress on billing system upgrades, including a new autopay feature launching the following week, and an update on the city's application to the state's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) program, which would provide recycling reimbursements to customers.

The most spirited debate centered on whether and how to launch a composting program. Staff recommended Option 3 — a near-term drop-off site pilot followed by composting as a bid option in the next hauler contract (due July 2028). Mayor Pro Tem Moorman and Councilmember Ambrose pushed for Option 2, a citywide opt-out curbside composting program, arguing EPR reimbursements could cover the cost, potentially making it a wash on customer bills. Other members cited concerns about staff capacity, community trust, and the complexity of launching multiple programs simultaneously. The workshop ended with a council consensus around Option 3 as the near-term direction, with the door left open to revisiting Option 2 as early as spring 2027.

key_decisions.motion: Excuse Councilmember Davis from the special meeting portion
key_decisions.significance: Davis was excused from voting on matters related to her own ethics investigation, consistent with standard conflict-of-interest practice.

key_decisions.motion: Authorize retention of outside investigator and outside counsel to pursue a second ethics investigation and administrative process involving Councilmember Sharon Davis under Arvada Code of Ethics
key_decisions.significance: This is the second ethics investigation authorized against Davis in under three months. The dissenting votes reflect a council split over process — specifically whether results of the first investigation should be disclosed before launching a second.

notable_quotes.speaker: Michael Griffith
notable_quotes.quote: I would very much like to see the findings of the first investigation to be shared prior to the opening of a new investigation. So I would not be supporting this motion.
notable_quotes.context: Griffith explaining his no vote on the second ethics investigation authorization, raising a process concern about sequencing.

notable_quotes.speaker: City Attorney Hoffman
notable_quotes.quote: My recommendation would be to actually authorize the investigation into the second claim because frankly it could have the effect of — if the first investigation is released now — of impacting the overall process of council.
notable_quotes.context: Hoffman advising council to consolidate both ethics investigations rather than releasing the first findings prematurely, which swayed Mayor Simpson's vote.

notable_quotes.speaker: Randy Moorman
notable_quotes.quote: The city of Arvada would have the lowest rate with a three-cart program in the entire state of Colorado.
notable_quotes.context: Moorman summarizing Republic Services rep Steve Gilmore's claim that EPR reimbursements could fully offset a new curbside composting program with no net cost increase to customers.

notable_quotes.speaker: Shawna Ambrose
notable_quotes.quote: This is also about wildfire defense because people will be able to reduce slash of their leaves, of sticks and other organics in an accessible way... you are only as safe as your neighbor.
notable_quotes.context: Ambrose making a public safety argument for Option 2 citywide curbside composting, linking organic waste pickup to wildfire risk reduction.

notable_quotes.speaker: Michael Griffith
notable_quotes.quote: This is a rare opportunity for government to provide the same service for a lower cost to our residents and we just don't get that opportunity very often.
notable_quotes.context: Griffith noting that doing nothing (Option 4) or Option 1 would result in customers saving $5–7/month via EPR reimbursements without adding a composting charge — a trade-off he felt deserved more discussion.

notable_quotes.speaker: City Manager (Don Wick)
notable_quotes.quote: What you just articulated is exactly our concern around — while we agree, like this looks like the perfect time — it's the community trust aspect of this that we're really really concerned about.
notable_quotes.context: City leadership explaining why staff recommended the slower Option 3 approach over the faster Option 2, citing the team's current workload including drought response.

notable_quotes.speaker: Bob Fifer
notable_quotes.quote: I hate to see the bill drop and then pick up because I think it's a harder hill to climb.
notable_quotes.context: Fifer voicing concern that showing customers an EPR credit and then adding a composting charge shortly after could erode public trust in the billing program.

topics_discussed: Ethics investigation (second authorization for Councilmember Davis)
Solid waste enterprise fund reserve balance
Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) program application and timeline
Billing system upgrades (autopay, online access, version upgrades)
Next hauler contract RFP timeline (toward July 2028)

consistency_flags.observation: Lovisone voted No on the second ethics investigation, citing a desire to see findings from the first investigation before opening a new one. However, she told the council she would 'also be voting no quickly' and in the same breath asked city attorney Hoffman for legal advice on whether proceeding was appropriate — suggesting her stated position and actual reasoning may have been forming in real time rather than based on a firm prior stance.

Fifer stated he supported all four staff recommendations — including Option 3 for composting — yet spent considerable time exploring Option 2 mechanics, suggesting a tiered pricing model, and asking 'what's the downside?' of the EPR-funded composting scenario. His ultimate stated support for Option 3 came after the city manager's intervention, raising a question about whether his support for Option 3 was a genuine first preference or a deference to staff capacity concerns.

on_the_horizon: The city is under Stage 1 drought restrictions (effective April 15, 2026), and staff mentioned a May presentation to council on potential water surcharges to financially incentivize conservation — worth watching for residents facing higher water bills. On the solid waste front, the formal rate adoption vote is scheduled for June 16, with new rates taking effect July 1; the June meeting will also be the moment to watch for any composting direction updates if the EPR master service agreement with Circular Action Alliance has progressed. The Arvada Summerfest on June 6 coincides with the next large-item drop-off event at the same Ralston Park Addition location.
